title: Codes, Standards, and Specifications
narrationScript: Every visual test you perform answers a question someone else asked first. An engineer decided that this weld or this vessel needed a visual examination, decided what counts as acceptable, and wrote those decisions down. Your job as a Level Two is to execute that examination exactly as written, and to judge the part against the written criteria. This lesson is about the paper trail that connects the two of you, because if you cannot find and read the governing documents, you cannot do this job. Start with the split between visual testing as an engineering task and visual testing as a technician task. Engineering owns the what and the why. Engineers select visual testing as the method, decide where and when it applies, set the acceptance criteria, and disposition anything that falls outside those criteria. Technicians own the how and the result. We verify the conditions. Lighting, access, surface preparation. We perform the examination to the written procedure. We interpret what we see, evaluate it against the acceptance criteria, and report it. Here is the line you never cross. A technician never invents and never relaxes acceptance criteria. If a part fails the written criteria but looks fine to you, it still fails. The path to accepting it runs through engineering, usually through a material review board or a formal concession. It never runs through your eraser. Next, learn the document stack. At the top sits law or regulation. In much of North America, jurisdictions adopt the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code into law. In Europe, the Pressure Equipment Directive is the law for most pressure equipment placed on the market. Below the law sit codes and standards. A code, such as ASME Section eight, tells you how to build a pressure vessel and what is acceptable. A standard, such as AWS D one point one, covers structural welding of steel. Below those sits the specification. That is the contract or purchase order language that tells you which code, which edition, and any extra requirements the customer added. At the bottom sits your employer's written procedure, the document you actually work to, which must satisfy everything above it. Inside the American system, there is one distinction you must carry everywhere. Method documents tell you how to look. Acceptance documents tell you what is acceptable. ASME Section five, Article nine, is a method document. It tells you how to write a visual examination procedure, how close your eye must be, within six hundred millimeters or twenty four inches, the minimum viewing angle of thirty degrees, and how to demonstrate that your procedure can resolve fine detail on the surface. It does not tell you whether undercut is acceptable. For that, you go to the referencing code. Section eight for pressure vessels, Section one for power boilers, B thirty one point three for process piping. AWS D one point one is different. It is largely self contained. The visual acceptance criteria for structural welds, the undercut limits, the porosity limits, the profile requirements, sit in a table inside the same document. Across the Atlantic, the logic is the same but the names change. The Pressure Equipment Directive sets essential safety requirements and requires CE marking before equipment goes to market. Harmonized European standards, such as EN one three four four five for unfired pressure vessels and EN one three four eight zero for metallic piping, give a presumption of conformity with the directive. For weld visual inspection, the method standard is ISO one seven six three seven, and acceptance is usually called out as a quality level from ISO five eight one seven. Level B is the most stringent, then C, then D. Personnel certification differs as well. The American practice under SNT TC one A is employer based. Your employer's written practice defines training, experience, and examinations, and your employer issues your certification. The ISO nine seven one two model is central certification through an accredited third party body. Under the Pressure Equipment Directive, permanent joints on the higher hazard categories of equipment must be tested by personnel approved by a recognized third party organization. So how do you find your acceptance criteria on a real job? Work down the stack. Read the contract or purchase order to find the governing code and its edition. Read the job traveler or inspection plan. Open your employer's procedure and confirm it invokes that same code and edition. Then go to the acceptance table and read it before you ever look at the part. The strongest habit a Level Two can build is simple. Never start an examination until you can state, out loud, which document and which paragraph you will accept or reject against.

sections.heading: Two Hats: Engineering Task vs. Technician Task
sections.body:
Visual testing shows up twice in the life of a component. First as an **engineering task**: a design or engineering authority decides that VT is the right method for the item, decides where and when it applies (in-process, final, in-service), writes or approves the specification, sets the acceptance criteria, and dispositions anything that fails them — repair, rework, use-as-is by formal concession, or scrap. Second as a **technician task**: a qualified examiner verifies the preconditions (correct procedure revision, lighting, access, surface condition, calibrated equipment), performs the examination per the written procedure, interprets what is seen, evaluates it against the stated acceptance criteria, and records the result.

The boundary matters. A Level II compares the part to the criteria — never the criteria to the part. You do not tighten criteria because a weld looks ugly, and you do not relax them because the schedule is tight. If an item fails but engineering believes it is fit for service, the acceptance path is a documented engineering disposition (for example, a material review board action), not a quiet pass from the examiner. Knowing which hat you wear — and which one you do not — is the foundation for everything else in this lesson.

sections.caption: Requirements flow down a fixed stack — law, code, standard, specification, procedure — with engineering owning the top of the stack and the technician executing the bottom.

sections.heading: The Document Stack
sections.body:
Field shorthand treats *code*, *standard*, and *specification* as interchangeable. They are not:

- **Regulation / law** — a jurisdiction's legal requirement. Many North American jurisdictions adopt the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code into law; in the EU, the Pressure Equipment Directive (PED) is law for most pressure equipment placed on the market.
- **Code** — a comprehensive body of rules suitable for adoption into law (ASME BPVC, piping codes).
- **Standard** — a consensus document defining how a task is done or what a product must achieve (AWS D1.1, EN ISO 17637).
- **Specification** — contract-level requirements: which code and which edition govern, plus any customer additions or restrictions.
- **Procedure** — your employer's controlled document telling you exactly how to execute the examination so everything above it is satisfied.

Requirements flow down this stack; they never flow up. Your procedure may be more restrictive than the code it serves, never less. When two invoked documents conflict, the contract usually states an order of precedence — and the answer to a conflict is a documented question to your Level III or engineering, not a guess at the workbench.

sections.heading: US Standards You Will Actually Meet
sections.body:
The ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code is organized by product: Section I (power boilers) and Section VIII (pressure vessels), with the B31 piping codes (B31.1 power, B31.3 process) alongside. These **construction codes own the acceptance criteria**. For how to examine, they reference Section V, *Nondestructive Examination*, whose Article 9 covers visual examination: what a written VT procedure must contain, demonstration that the procedure resolves fine surface detail, direct viewing with the eye within 600 mm (24 in.) of the surface at not less than 30°, and remote VT with resolution at least equivalent to direct viewing.

AWS D1.1, the structural welding code for steel, behaves differently: it is largely self-contained, carrying its own visual acceptance table — undercut limits, porosity limits, profile requirements, prohibition of cracks — so the examiner works method and acceptance from one book. API documents (510 for pressure vessels, 570 for piping, 653 for storage tanks) govern in-service inspection, where VT is the workhorse for corrosion, distortion, and mechanical damage, and API 1104 covers pipeline girth welds. ASTM standards supply supporting practices and reference photographs. The pattern to internalize: identify the construction or in-service code first, then follow its pointers to method and acceptance.

sections.caption: Where VT method requirements and acceptance criteria live in commonly invoked documents.
rows: How to examine: procedure content, 600 mm / 30° direct viewing, lighting, resolution demonstration
Method only — acceptance comes from the referencing code
ASME Section VIII / Section I / B31.3
Construction rules for vessels, boilers, process piping
Self-contained visual acceptance table inside D1.1
Evaluation rules for in-service damage in each API document
Unfired vessels and metallic piping in the EU
Method per EN ISO 17637; acceptance as ISO 5817 quality levels B, C, D
Employer-based written practice vs. central third-party certification

sections.caption: The US and EU frameworks solve the same problem with different documents: law at the top, acceptance criteria in the construction standard, method in a referenced examination standard, and personnel rules at the base.

sections.heading: The European Framework: PED and EN-ISO
sections.body:
The Pressure Equipment Directive (2014/68/EU) is EU law: it states *essential safety requirements* and requires CE marking before most pressure equipment is placed on the EU market. The PED contains no weld acceptance tables. Instead, **harmonized standards** — EN 13445 for unfired pressure vessels, EN 13480 for metallic industrial piping — provide a *presumption of conformity*: design, build, and inspect to them and you are presumed to meet the directive.

For weld VT, the method standard is EN ISO 17637 (visual testing of fusion-welded joints), and acceptance is normally specified as a **quality level from EN ISO 5817**: level B (most stringent), C, or D, selected by the design standard or the contract. Aluminum welds use the parallel ISO 10042.

Personnel rules differ sharply from US practice. SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice: the employer writes a written practice and certifies its own people. EN ISO 9712 is central certification: an accredited third-party body examines and certifies the individual, who carries that certification between employers. Under the PED, NDT of permanent joints on category III and IV equipment must be performed by personnel approved by a recognized third-party organization — which is why ISO 9712-style certification is effectively mandatory for that work.

sections.title: Method is not acceptance
sections.body: Documents such as ASME Section V, Article 9 tell you **how** to examine — procedure content, viewing distance and angle, lighting, demonstration of resolution. They do not tell you what is acceptable. Acceptance criteria live in the referencing construction code, the contract specification, or a self-contained standard such as AWS D1.1. Quoting a method document as your accept/reject basis is a finding waiting to happen in your next audit.

sections.heading: Finding the Requirements on a Real Job
sections.body: Make this a fixed routine. **One:** read the contract, purchase order, or job traveler and write down the governing code or standard *and its edition year* — editions change acceptance values, and the applicable edition is the one stated in the contract or the jurisdiction's adoption, not the newest one on the shelf. **Two:** open your employer's VT procedure and confirm it invokes that document and edition; if it does not, stop and raise it. **Three:** locate the actual acceptance paragraph or table and read it before examining — note the units, the weld categories, and any thickness-dependent limits. **Four:** check for customer flow-downs that tighten the code values; the contract's order-of-precedence clause says which wins. **Five:** record document, edition, and paragraph on your report so a stranger can reconstruct your acceptance basis years later. Auditors call this *traceability of the acceptance basis*, and missing it is among the most common VT report findings. Ten minutes with the paperwork prevents the worst outcome in this trade: a technically perfect examination evaluated against the wrong numbers.

figures.title: The Document Stack: Law to Work Instruction
Two Frameworks, One Job: US vs. EU/ISO

checkQuestions.question: Setting the acceptance criteria for a visual examination is whose responsibility?
options: The Level II performing the examination
The design or engineering authority that specifies the work
The welder who produced the joint
The light meter manufacturer
checkQuestions.explanation: Selecting VT as the method and defining what is acceptable are engineering tasks; the technician evaluates against those criteria but never creates or relaxes them.

checkQuestions.question: Your procedure references ASME Section V, Article 9 for visual examination of a pressure vessel weld. Where will you usually find the acceptance criteria?
options: In Article 9 itself
In the referencing construction code, such as Section VIII
In SNT-TC-1A
In the employer's calibration log
checkQuestions.explanation: Article 9 is a method document — it tells you how to examine. Acceptance criteria live in the construction code that referenced it, such as Section VIII for pressure vessels.

checkQuestions.question: Under the European PED framework, weld VT acceptance is most commonly specified as:
options: A quality level (B, C, or D) from EN ISO 5817
A footcandle minimum listed in the PED annexes
An AWS D1.1 table invoked by CE marking
Whatever level the fabricator's Level III prefers
checkQuestions.explanation: EN ISO 17637 supplies the examination method; acceptance is called out as an ISO 5817 quality level, with B the most stringent, then C, then D.

checkQuestions.question: The key difference between EN ISO 9712 and SNT-TC-1A certification is that:
options: ISO 9712 covers visual testing only
ISO 9712 is central third-party certification, while SNT-TC-1A is an employer-based recommended practice
SNT-TC-1A requires no examinations of any kind
ISO 9712 has no experience requirements
checkQuestions.explanation: Under ISO 9712 an accredited third-party body certifies the individual; under SNT-TC-1A the employer defines requirements in a written practice and issues the certification itself.

checkQuestions.question: Before starting a visual examination on a new job, the most reliable way to identify the governing code edition is to:
options: Default to the latest published edition
Check the contract documents, purchase order, or job traveler
Ask the welder which edition was used
Use the edition you were originally trained on
checkQuestions.explanation: The applicable edition is the one invoked by the contract or the jurisdiction's adoption — editions change acceptance values, so you trace the requirement, never assume it.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II §9.1–9.5
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 — visual examination method (generic reference)
ASME BPVC Sections I and VIII; ASME B31.1 / B31.3 construction codes (generic reference)
AWS D1.1 Structural Welding Code — Steel (generic reference)
Pressure Equipment Directive 2014/68/EU; EN 13445; EN ISO 17637; EN ISO 5817 (generic reference)
SNT-TC-1A and EN ISO 9712 personnel certification frameworks (generic reference)
